---
title: "Sentinel-2 MSI for Urban Remote Sensing"
subtitle: "CASA0023 Week 2 Learning Diary Presentation"
author: "Zhexiang Zhou"
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    lib_dir: libs
    css: ["default", "metropolis", "metropolis-fonts", "styles.css"]
    nature:
      ratio: "16:9"
      highlightStyle: github
      highlightLines: true
      countIncrementalSlides: false
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE)
```

class: center, middle, hero
background-image: url("images/london_s2.jpg")
background-size: cover
background-position: center

# Sentinel-2 MSI for Urban Decisions
## Sensor summary, real applications, and personal reflection

### Focused on the CASA0023 mark scheme

---

# 1) Sensor Summary: What Sentinel-2 MSI Is

.pull-left[
.img-frame[
![Sentinel-2 mission event image](images/sentinel2a_launch.jpg)
]

.tiny-note[
Image: ESA/J. Mai via Wikimedia Commons (CC BY-SA IGO 3.0)
]
]

.pull-right[
<div class="card">
<span class="accent">Sentinel-2 MSI</span> is a passive multispectral optical sensor designed for land and urban surface monitoring.
</div>

<ul class="tight">
<li>13 spectral bands (VIS, red-edge, NIR, SWIR)</li>
<li>Spatial resolution: 10 m / 20 m / 60 m</li>
<li>Swath width: ~290 km</li>
<li>Revisit: ~5 days with Sentinel-2A + 2B</li>
<li>Open and free Copernicus data access</li>
</ul>

.small-note[
Core value: frequent, open, and spectrally rich observations for city-scale analysis.
]
]

---

# 2) Independent Summary: Strengths and Constraints

.pull-left[
### Strengths
<div class="card">
<ul class="tight">
<li>Strong land-cover separability for built-up, vegetation, and water.</li>
<li>Red-edge bands improve urban vegetation analysis.</li>
<li>High revisit frequency supports change monitoring.</li>
<li>Open archive enables reproducible workflows and low-cost scaling.</li>
</ul>
</div>
]

.pull-right[
### Constraints
<div class="card-warm">
<ul class="tight">
<li>No thermal band: limited for direct land-surface temperature retrieval.</li>
<li>Optical data quality drops under cloud and haze.</li>
<li>10 m pixels can mix materials in dense urban blocks.</li>
<li>Street-level object detection requires much finer resolution.</li>
</ul>
</div>
]

.small-note[
Independent judgement: Sentinel-2 is excellent as a <span class="accent">baseline urban sensor</span>, and often strongest when fused with thermal or SAR data.
]

---

# 3) Application Scope in Urban Remote Sensing

.pull-left[
### Common urban uses in the literature
<ul class="tight">
<li><span class="accent">Land-cover classification</span> (built-up, green, water, bare land)</li>
<li><span class="accent">Urban growth tracking</span> using multi-date scenes</li>
<li><span class="accent">Green infrastructure mapping</span> with red-edge and NIR features</li>
<li><span class="accent">Interpretable ML</span> for policy-facing evidence</li>
</ul>
]

.pull-right[
### Analytical framing for the case studies
<div class="card">
<ul class="tight">
<li><b>Problem focus:</b> what urban question each study addresses</li>
<li><b>Method strategy:</b> model and feature choices with Sentinel-2</li>
<li><b>Evidence quality:</b> where the approach is strong and where limits remain</li>
<li><b>Practice value:</b> how findings can support urban planning decisions</li>
</ul>
</div>

]

---

# 4) Study A: Optimized Random Forest Classification

.pull-left[
### Zhang et al. (2021), *Applied Sciences*

<ul class="study">
<li><b>Purpose:</b> improve urban land-cover classification from Sentinel-2 data.</li>
<li><b>Method:</b> optimized Random Forest using Sentinel-2 feature sets.</li>
<li><b>Result relevance:</b> robust accuracy can be achieved with careful feature and parameter selection.</li>
</ul>

<div class="study-note">
My comment: good evidence for practical mapping, but transferability across different cities still needs local validation.
</div>
]

.pull-right[
.img-frame[
![Sentinel-2 Chicago scene](images/chicago_s2.jpg)
]

.tiny-note[
Image: Chicago by Sentinel-2, Copernicus attribution via Wikimedia Commons
]
]

---

# 5) Study B: Spectro-Spatio-Temporal Urban Mapping

.pull-left[
### Xu, Heremans & Somers (2022), *Urban Informatics*

<ul class="study">
<li><b>Purpose:</b> test spectral, spatial, and temporal strategies for urban land-cover mapping.</li>
<li><b>Method:</b> compare single-date and time-series Sentinel-2 classifier setups.</li>
<li><b>Result relevance:</b> combining spring and summer observations improved class discrimination.</li>
</ul>

<div class="study-note">
My comment: acquisition timing is not just technical detail, it directly changes classification quality.
</div>
]

.pull-right[
.img-frame[
![Sentinel-2 London scene](images/london_s2.jpg)
]

.tiny-note[
Image: London by Sentinel-2, Copernicus attribution via Wikimedia Commons
]
]

---

# 6) Study C: Interpretable Machine Learning for LULC

.pull-left[
### Hosseiny, Abdi & Jamali (2022), *RSASE*

<ul class="study">
<li><b>Purpose:</b> compare shallow and deep models for urban LULC classification.</li>
<li><b>Method:</b> 141 features, 85 scenarios, class-level SHAP interpretation.</li>
<li><b>Result relevance:</b> deep models achieved higher mean accuracy and clearer class-level explanations.</li>
</ul>

<div class="study-note">
My comment: interpretability is critical when outputs are used by planners, not only technical analysts.
</div>
]

.pull-right[
.img-frame[
![Sentinel-2 Manhattan scene](images/manhattan_s2.jpg)
]

.tiny-note[
Image: Manhattan by Sentinel-2, Copernicus attribution via Wikimedia Commons
]
]

---

# 7) Cross-Study Comparison (Why It Matters)

Across the three studies, one pattern is clear: stronger urban classification comes from combining model design, temporal strategy, and interpretability.

<table class="matrix-table">
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Study</th>
      <th>What it adds</th>
      <th>Why it matters for urban analysis</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>Zhang et al. (2021)</td>
      <td>Optimized RF with Sentinel-2 features</td>
      <td>Use as a reliable baseline before trying heavier models</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Xu et al. (2022)</td>
      <td>Spectro-spatio-temporal design</td>
      <td>Prioritize season-aware image selection, not only model tuning</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Hosseiny et al. (2022)</td>
      <td>Interpretable shallow/deep comparison</td>
      <td>Pair higher accuracy with explainability for planning decisions</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

<div class="card">
Synthesis: Sentinel-2 performs best when workflows combine <b>baseline robustness</b>, <b>time-aware data design</b>, and <b>interpretable modelling</b>.
</div>

---

# 8) Personal Reflection and Future Work

<div class="card">
I learned that sensor choice is a methodological decision, not a default. Sentinel-2 is powerful because it is open, frequent, and spectrally rich, but I must design around its optical and spatial limits.
</div>

### What I learned
<ul class="tight">
<li>Classification quality depends on feature selection, timing, and validation.</li>
<li>Urban results become more useful when model outputs are interpretable.</li>
<li>Limitations (cloud, mixed pixels, no thermal) must be explicit in analysis reporting.</li>
</ul>

### How I would use this in future work
<ul class="tight">
<li>Use Sentinel-2 as a baseline layer for city land-cover and vegetation dynamics.</li>
<li>Fuse with thermal/SAR data for UHI or cloudy-season analysis.</li>
<li>Build repeatable multi-date workflows for stronger evidence in planning contexts.</li>
</ul>

---

# References

**Core sensor sources**

- Copernicus Sentinel-2 mission page: https://sentinels.copernicus.eu/web/sentinel/missions/sentinel-2
- Copernicus media page: https://www.copernicus.eu/en/media/images/sentinel-2-1
- Drusch, M. et al. (2012). Sentinel-2: ESA's optical high-resolution mission for GMES operational services. *Remote Sensing of Environment*, 120, 25-36. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.rse.2011.11.026

**Application studies**

- Zhang, T. et al. (2021). Sentinel-2 Satellite Imagery for Urban Land Cover Classification by Optimized Random Forest Classifier. *Applied Sciences*, 11(2), 543. https://doi.org/10.3390/app11020543
- Xu, F., Heremans, S., & Somers, B. (2022). Urban land cover mapping with Sentinel-2: a spectro-spatio-temporal analysis. *Urban Informatics*, 1, 8. https://doi.org/10.1007/s44212-022-00008-y
- Hosseiny, B., Abdi, A. M., & Jamali, S. (2022). Urban land use and land cover classification with interpretable machine learning: A case study using Sentinel-2 and auxiliary data. *Remote Sensing Applications: Society and Environment*, 28, 100843. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.rsase.2022.100843

**Image credits**

- ESA/J. Mai (Sentinel-2A launch at ESOC), via Wikimedia Commons.
- Copernicus Sentinel-2 imagery (Chicago, London, Manhattan), via Wikimedia Commons.
